Hardware-based isolation
Protects LSASS credentials
Virtualization-based security

Target unprotected sources:
Memory of other processes
SAM database, NTDS.dit
LSA Secrets

Kerberoasting:
Service ticket extraction
Doesn't require LSASS access
Offline cracking

Steal Kerberos tickets:
klist sessions
Rubeus dump /luid
Export .kirbi files

NTLM relay attacks
Pass-the-Hash over network
Doesn't require local creds

Obtain credentials
without Credential Guard bypass

Whitelist execution policy
Block unauthorized executables

Write to allowed paths:
C:\\Windows\\Tasks
C:\\Windows\\Temp
C:\\Windows\\tracing

Hide executable in ADS:
type payload.exe > file.txt:hidden.exe
wmic process call create C:\\file.txt:hidden.exe

Use allowed script interpreters:
PowerShell, VBScript, JScript
If not explicitly blocked

Place malicious DLL
next to trusted executable
DLL search order hijacking

Use Microsoft-signed binaries:
msbuild.exe, csc.exe
regsvcs.exe, InstallUtil.exe

String encoding:
Base64, XOR, AES
Variable renaming
Dead code insertion

Change code structure:
Recompile with variations
Different encryption keys
Randomized execution flow

Living-off-the-land binaries:
certutil, bitsadmin
mshta, regsvr32
Trusted signed binaries

Inject into legitimate process:
explorer.exe, svchost.exe
DLL injection, Process hollowing
APC injection

Sleep before execution
Delayed execution
Avoid sandbox detection
Time-based triggers

Bypass AV/EDR detection
Execute malicious code

Security, System, Application logs
Record authentication, process creation

wevtutil cl Security
Clear-EventLog
Suspicious but effective

Remove specific Event IDs:
4624 logon, 4688 process
Using Remove-EventLog or wevtutil

Modify log file directly:
Parse .evtx format
Remove incriminating entries

In-memory execution only:
Reflective DLL injection
Fileless malware
Living-off-the-land

Reduced forensic evidence
Harder to detect actions

CLR ETW provider logs
.NET and PowerShell activity

Patch EtwEventWrite:
Modify ntdll.dll function
in memory to return early

Inject into non-monitored process
Execute from there
Avoid instrumented processes

Activity not logged
to ETW traces

PowerShell script scanning
Detects malicious patterns

Patch amsi.dll in memory:
[Ref].Assembly.GetType
AmsiUtils.AmsiInitFailed = true

String concatenation:
I`E`X, Invoke-Expression
Base64 encoding, XOR

Use .NET reflection:
Bypass AMSI context
Direct system calls

Use C#, VBScript, JScript
Rundll32, regsvr32
Avoid PowerShell detection

Use encryption for C2 traffic
DNS tunneling, HTTPS

Blend with normal traffic
Use standard ports
Mimic legitimate processes

Clean up artifacts:
Delete files, clear logs
Remove scheduled tasks

BLUE TEAM DETECTION:
Monitor PowerShell ScriptBlock logging Despite AMSI bypass
Collect ETW from multiple providers Redundant logging
Centralize logs to SIEM Immediate forwarding prevents deletion
File Integrity Monitoring Alert on amsi.dll ntdll.dll modification
Behavioral detection ML/AI for anomalies vs signatures

ATTACKER BEST PRACTICES:
Layer evasion techniques AMSI + ETW + Obfuscation
Test in isolated environment Verify evasion before operation
Use custom tools Avoid public/signatured tools
Minimize noise Targeted over broad scans
